content_markdown:
The graph below shows the variation with absolute temperature $T$ of the pressure $p$ of one mole of an ideal gas having a volume $V$. $R$ is the molar gas constant.

Which of the following is the best interpretation of the intercept on the temperature axis and the gradient of the graph?

diagram_metadata.description: A graph showing pressure p on the y-axis and absolute temperature T on the x-axis. A straight line with a positive gradient is shown in the first quadrant, which if extrapolated backwards would point towards the origin.

options.label: A
options.text: Intercept on temperature axis / K: $-273$; Gradient of graph: $\frac{R}{V}$

options.label: B
options.text: Intercept on temperature axis / K: $0$; Gradient of graph: $\frac{R}{V}$

options.label: C
options.text: Intercept on temperature axis / K: $0$; Gradient of graph: $\frac{V}{R}$

options.label: D
options.text: Intercept on temperature axis / K: $-273$; Gradient of graph: $\frac{V}{R}$

vibe_explanation: According to the ideal gas law $pV = nRT$, for one mole ($n=1$), we have $p = \frac{R}{V}T$. This is in the form $y = mx + c$ where $y=p$ and $x=T$. Since $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin, the pressure $p$ is directly proportional to $T$, meaning the line passes through the origin $(0,0)$. Therefore, the intercept on the temperature axis is $0$ K. The gradient $m$ is the coefficient of $T$, which is $\frac{R}{V}$.
